project_description.text: The Eastwick Urban Renewal Area is a 2,506-acre redevelopment project in Southwest Philadelphia, run by the Philadelphia Redevelopment Authority since 1958, that is transforming former marsh and substandard land near the Schuylkill and Delaware Rivers into a planned 'New Town' within the city. The plan covers four residential stages and three industrial stages, including row houses, apartments, shopping centers, schools, churches, parks, and a proposed Mini-Town Center near Island Avenue and Lindbergh Boulevard. Land acquisition is complete, about 1,550 acres remain to be conveyed, and the New Eastwick Corporation (Korman Corporation) is the main residential developer, with site work including roughly 1.5 million cubic yards of landfill begun in 1968 and street and sewer construction phased through 1979.

affected_community.text: The 1970 Census counted about 15,977 residents in Eastwick (roughly 87% white), with 5,006 housing units and a median family income of $9,283; full buildout is projected to bring the population to about 33,000. New residents tend to be younger, middle and upper-middle income, and more racially mixed than the older established community, which lives mainly in row houses north of the project. Since 1959 the Authority has relocated about 2,400 families, 325 individuals, and 207 businesses, and the Eastwick Project Area Committee, representing eight neighborhood and business groups, provides organized citizen input on planning decisions.

alternatives_overview.text: Three broad alternatives were weighed: doing nothing (rejected because the area would remain marshy and substandard), expanding clearance and rehabilitation (limited to avoid added relocation and cost), and changing land uses in problem areas. The most significant change is converting Stage III from residential to airport-related commercial use because of flooding, air quality, and aircraft noise, with HUD tentatively approving the change in 1971 and a 50-acre motel-commercial complex recommended. Other options considered include raising grades above the 100-year flood, building dikes to protect residential areas, dedicating the abandoned I-695 right-of-way as open space or an addition to the Tinicum Wildlife Preserve, and replanning Stage IV streets to reduce noise impacts.

environmental_impact.text: The project eliminates marshy lowlands and wildlife habitat, contributes sediment to Darby and Cobbs Creeks and the adjacent Tinicum Marsh Preserve (with over four feet of siltation at the Tinicum flood gates since 1969), and leaves parts of the area exposed to 100-year tidal flooding at about elevation 10–11 feet, requiring proposed dikes and surge basin changes. Noise from aircraft (NEF 30–40 contours), I-95 and other major roads, and the Reading Railroad and Airport High Speed Line requires soundproofing, setbacks, and 20–35 dBA noise reduction in construction, while air pollution analyses show Stage III cannot meet standards for residential use. Additional concerns include soil settlement, methane from former dumps such as Heller's and the Clearview Landfill, raw sewage overflows into Darby Creek from combined sewers, septic discharges, and a high water table requiring special sewer materials and erosion controls.

public_response.text: Federal agencies (Interior, EPA, Army Corps of Engineers, DOT/FAA, Commerce) and state agencies (PennDOT, PA DER) raised concerns about creek sedimentation, ground water and air quality, historic resources such as the John Bartram House, and the wisdom of building more housing inside airport noise contours, with several urging major changes to Stages III and IV. Local bodies including the City Planning Commission, SEPTA, and the Korman Corporation generally supported continued development with mitigation and backed the Stage III commercial conversion, while the Redevelopment Authority disputed some erosion and air quality findings. The Eastwick Project Area Committee, along with longtime residents who had historically opposed redevelopment, pressed for stronger air and noise controls, sewer extensions to excluded homes, completion of storm drainage, dredging of Darby Creek, and remedies for basement flooding, odors, and rats from Heller's Dump.

value.name: Do Nothing Alternative
value.description: Taking no action on the Eastwick area, which would have left much of the land in a marsh condition with dilapidated and substandard structures, making development by private enterprise extremely unlikely.

value.name: Greater Degree of Clearance/Greater Degree of Rehabilitation
value.description: Consideration of additional demolition of standard but aesthetically incompatible structures (e.g., older brick residences in Upper Stage II and a 1950s apartment complex in Lower Stage II) to achieve design cohesiveness, weighed against increased relocation costs and community relations impacts.

value.name: Changes in Land Use Proposals
value.description: Examination of alternative land uses for undeveloped stages, including converting Stage III from residential to commercial/airport-related use, changing noise-impacted parcels in Stages I and II to parks or less sensitive uses, and designating the former I-695 right-of-way and Stage C areas as park/open space buffering the Tinicum Preserve.

subthemes: Urban Expansion and Land Use Change

themes.justification: The project_description and environmental_impact fields describe a large-scale urban renewal effort converting marshy lowlands into a planned residential/commercial 'New Town,' with significant concerns about flooding, sewer infrastructure, creek sedimentation, and destruction of wetland habitat including the adjacent Tinicum Marsh Preserve.

places.name: Eastwick Urban Renewal Area, Philadelphia, Pennsylvania

geocoded.name: Eastwick Urban Renewal Area, Philadelphia, Pennsylvania

location.note: 2,506-acre urban renewal area in Southwest Philadelphia (40th Ward), bounded by 58th Street (NE), Essington Avenue (E), Industrial Highway (S), and Delaware County Line/Passyunk/Dicks Avenues (W/NW), adjacent to Philadelphia International Airport.

agency_preparers.name: Philadelphia Area Office, Region III, U.S. Department of Housing and Urban Development

cooperating_agencies.name: U.S. Department of the Army - Corps of Engineers
U.S. Department of Transportation - Regional Representative
Commonwealth of Pennsylvania - Department of Transportation
Commonwealth of Pennsylvania - Department of Environmental Resources
City of Philadelphia - City Representative and Director of Commerce
City of Philadelphia - Air Management Services
Redevelopment Authority of the City of Philadelphia

public_commenters.name: United States Department of Commerce
public_commenters.quote: We believe that the statement should discuss which plans are being considered. Further, the Redevelopment Authority should consult with appropriate state and federal agencies during early planning stages in an effort to develop the most environmentally acceptable plan.

public_commenters.name: United States Department of Transportation, Federal Highway Administration
public_commenters.quote: We have no significant comments from a highway standpoint.

public_commenters.name: Department of Army, Philadelphia District, Corps of Engineers
public_commenters.quote: Statement does not document fact sources.

public_commenters.name: United States Department of the Interior, Office of the Secretary
public_commenters.quote: we urge that this open space be designated for park and recreation purposes, and to enhance the adjacent Tinicum Preserve. The final statement should contain evidence of further consideration and consultation with city officials on this possibility.

public_commenters.name: United States Environmental Protection Agency, Region III
public_commenters.quote: While the draft statement has provided an adequate qualitative description of the project and potential related environmental impacts, EPA has concerns about certain of those impacts on air quality where applicable standards may be exceeded: the final statement must quantify these impacts and specify mitigating measures in further detail in order to adequately demonstrate compliance.
public_commenters.note: Quote not found verbatim — flagged for HUMAN_REVIEW

public_commenters.name: Pennsylvania Department of Transportation
public_commenters.quote: This Department concurs with the Redevelopment Authority, and, accordingly, air pollution and noise assessments have not been prepared for I-695.
public_commenters.note: Quote not found verbatim — flagged for HUMAN_REVIEW

public_commenters.name: Korman Corporation
public_commenters.quote: The positive position taken with respect to the renewal plan is noted.

public_commenters.name: Eastwick Project Area Committee
public_commenters.quote: Most of the comments contained in the letter from the Project Area Committee have been addressed in the rewritten Sections of Air Quality, Noise and Flooding.
